content.text: Measurement involves comparing an unknown quantity with a known, fixed quantity called a unit. A measurement is expressed in two parts: a number and a unit (e.g., 13 handspans).
Earlier units (handspan, cubit, foot, angula, stride) varied between individuals, causing confusion. This led to the adoption of the International System of Units (SI units).

content.title: History of Indian Measurement
content.text: Ancient India used units like angula (finger width), dhanusa, and yojana. Sites of the Harappan Civilisation have yielded excavated objects with ruled markings, suggesting the use of sophisticated scales.
The SI unit of length is the metre (m). Standard sub-units and multiples are essential for different types of measurement.

items: 1 kilometre (km) = 1000 m (for larger distances like cities).
1 centimetre (cm) = 10 mm (for thickness of a coin/pencil).

content.text: Non-SI units still in use: 1 inch = 2.54 cm. Foot is also a commonly used unit.

content.title: Example 1: Km to M conversion
content.description: The distance between home and school is 1.5 km. Express it in metres.

content.title: Example 2: M to Cm and Mm conversion
content.description: Express a friend's height of 1.65 metres in centimetres and millimetres.
content.solution: 1 m = 100 cm, so 1.65 m = 165 cm. Since 1 cm = 10 mm, 165 cm = 1650 mm.

content.title: Example 3: Inch to Cm conversion
content.description: The thickness of a wooden board is 2 inches. What is it in centimetres?
content.solution: 1 inch = 2.54 cm. Thus, 2 inches = 2 × 2.54 cm = 5.08 cm.

content.text: Accuracy in measurement requires following specific protocols to avoid parallax error and physical measurement mistakes.

content.title: Rules for Measurement
items: Placement: Scale must be in contact and parallel to the object along its length.
Eye Position: Position eye 'B' directly above the marking to avoid parallax (wrong readings from side angles).
Flexible Materials: Tailor's tape or a thread is used for measuring girth (tree trunk/chest size) where a straight scale cannot bend.
Aids for Visually Challenged: Special scales with raised markings are used so they can feel the length by touch.

content.title: Example 1: Subtraction Method
content.description: One end of a pencil aligns with 1.0 cm and the other with 10.4 cm on a broken scale. What is the actual length?
content.solution: Actual length = Final reading - Initial reading = 10.4 cm - 1.0 cm = 9.4 cm.

content.title: Example 2: Start from Middle Mark
content.description: A notebook's width is measured starting from the 3.5 cm mark. The final reading is 21.0 cm. Find the width.

notes.section_title: Curved Lines and Advanced Distance Measurement

content.text: Linear scales cannot directly measure curved lines. Activity 5.4 teaches us to use a thread: align it with the curve, mark the boundaries on the thread, and then measure the straightened thread on a metre scale.

content.title: Example 1: Calculating Distance from Rotations
content.description: A bicycle wheel's outer boundary (circumference) is 1.2 metres. If the wheel makes 100 full turns, how far did the bicycle travel?
content.solution: Distance = Circumference × Number of turns = 1.2 m × 100 = 120 metres.

content.title: Example 2: Measurement via Rotation Sound
content.description: A metal strip hits the frame once every wheel turn. You count 50 hits. If the wheel boundary is 2 metres, find the distance.
content.solution: Distance = 50 hits × 2 metres per hit = 100 metres.

content.title: Jones Counter
content.text: A specialized device attached to a bicycle wheel used to professionally measure road-running race distances based on this rotation principle.
Position is stated with respect to a fixed object or point called the reference point (e.g., kiln stones indicate distance from a city terminal like Delhi).
Motion is defined as the change in an object's position with respect to the reference point over time.
Rest is defined as the state where an object's position does not change over time.
Classification is based on the path follow by an object:

items: Linear Motion: Path follows a straight line (e.g., march-past, falling orange).
Circular Motion: Path follows a circular route (e.g., whirling a stone on thread, merry-go-round).
Oscillatory Motion: To-and-fro motion about a fixed position (e.g., swinging pendulum, vibrating metal strip).
Periodic Motion: Motion that repeats itself over a fixed interval. Circular and oscillatory movements are usually periodic.

glossary.definition: Handspan (unit of length derived from the thumb-to-little-finger distance).
Ancient Indian unit based on finger width.
Inaccurate reading caused by looking at a scale from an incorrect side angle.
Also known as rectilinear motion, occurs when an object follows a straight-line path.
A fixed location used to signify coordinate positions or change in motion.

summary_points: Standardized SI units like metre (m) ensure uniform measurement globally.
Accuracy depends on correct scale placement and avoiding parallax by direct eye-positioning.
Complex boundaries are measured using thread-alignment or wheel-rotation counting.
Motion is the position change relative to a fixed reference point over time.
Motion types include Linear, Circular, Oscillatory, and Periodic.
